text.title: Fine-Tuning LoRA: Training Configuration and Data Quality
text.summary: Hyperparameter selection, learning rate schedules, data quality rules, catastrophic forgetting prevention, and eval gate requirements.

rule.statement: Use learning_rate=2e-4 for QLoRA and 1e-4 for full LoRA as starting points. Apply cosine learning rate schedule with 3-5% warmup steps. Do not use linear decay — it converges slower and overshoots the minimum on small datasets.
rule.rationale: LoRA adapters have fewer parameters than full fine-tuning, so they can tolerate higher learning rates. QLoRA's 4-bit base weights have different gradient behavior — 2e-4 compensates for quantization noise. Cosine decay provides smooth convergence and avoids the sharp drop of linear decay that causes instability in the final training phase.

example.code:
from transformers import TrainingArguments
training_args = TrainingArguments( output_dir="./checkpoints", num_train_epochs=3, per_device_train_batch_size=1, gradient_accumulation_steps=8, # effective batch size = 8 learning_rate=2e-4, # QLoRA default lr_scheduler_type="cosine", warmup_ratio=0.03, # 3% warmup bf16=True, # bf16 for adapter weights gradient_checkpointing=True, logging_steps=10, save_strategy="epoch", eval_strategy="epoch", load_best_model_at_end=True, )

rule.statement: Minimum 200 examples to see measurable improvement; 1,000+ for reliable task specialization. Below 100 examples, use few-shot prompting instead. Quality gates: no duplicate examples (exact or near-duplicate), no truncated outputs, no mismatched instruction-response pairs.
rule.rationale: Transformers learn patterns from repetition. Below 100 examples, the model memorizes rather than generalizes. Duplicate examples inflate validation metrics while degrading real-world performance. Truncated outputs (cut at max_length mid-sentence) teach the model incomplete patterns that surface at inference.

rule.statement: Format all training examples in the same chat template as the target inference setup. Mismatch between training and inference format is the most common cause of degraded fine-tuned model quality.
rule.rationale: Chat templates (ChatML, Llama-3 template, Mistral template) add special tokens that the model uses to identify roles. If training uses raw text and inference uses a chat template, the model sees tokens it was never trained to produce, causing incoherent completions.

example.code:
from trl import SFTTrainer, DataCollatorForCompletionOnlyLM
# Only train on completion tokens, not the instruction response_template = "<|assistant|>" collator = DataCollatorForCompletionOnlyLM(response_template, tokenizer=tokenizer)
trainer = SFTTrainer( model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer, train_dataset=train_dataset, data_collator=collator, dataset_text_field="text", max_seq_length=2048, args=training_args, peft_config=lora_config, )

rule.statement: Mix 20-30% general instruction data (e.g., Alpaca, FLAN) into domain-specific training data. Eval on both domain and general benchmarks after training. If general benchmark drops more than 5%, reduce domain data ratio.
rule.rationale: Fine-tuning on a narrow task overwrites general capabilities. The model "forgets" how to follow general instructions, perform reasoning outside the training domain, or maintain output format. Mixing general data acts as a regularizer — the model must maintain general performance while learning the new task.

rule.statement: Use early stopping with patience=3 epochs. Monitor validation loss on the domain dataset; halt when it stops improving. Never train for a fixed number of epochs without monitoring — overfitting degrades generalization silently.
rule.rationale: LoRA adapters can overfit small datasets within 3-5 epochs. The training loss continues to decrease even as the validation loss starts rising. Without early stopping, the final checkpoint is the most overfitted — not the best. Load the best checkpoint (lowest validation loss) at the end.

rule.statement: Do not deploy a fine-tuned model without passing a quality gate: (1) task-specific accuracy above baseline + 10%, (2) general instruction following score above 80% of base model, (3) no refusal on in-distribution prompts (>95% response rate). Block deployment if any gate fails.
rule.rationale: Fine-tuned models frequently degrade in unexpected ways: they become more refusal-prone, lose general formatting, or hallucinate domain entities not present in training data. Without explicit gates, degraded models reach production and harm user trust. The baseline+10% threshold prevents deploying a fine-tuned model that underperforms the original.

example.code:
def eval_gate(model, tokenizer, domain_eval, general_eval, base_scores): domain_acc = evaluate_domain(model, tokenizer, domain_eval) general_score = evaluate_general(model, tokenizer, general_eval) refusal_rate = measure_refusal_rate(model, tokenizer, domain_eval)
assert domain_acc >= base_scores["domain"] + 0.10, \ f"Domain accuracy {domain_acc:.2f} below threshold {base_scores['domain']+0.10:.2f}" assert general_score >= base_scores["general"] * 0.80, \ f"General score {general_score:.2f} degraded below 80% of base {base_scores['general']:.2f}" assert refusal_rate <= 0.05, \ f"Refusal rate {refusal_rate:.2%} exceeds 5% threshold" return True
